\section{Introduction}
\setresponsible{Regen Wijaya}
%Recycling remains a crucial topic of global discussion and research. It not only helps reduce the waste generated by human, which often contributes to pollution, but also creates opportunities for positive impacts in other sector. Biomass residues, when thermally decomposed through Pyrolysis under restricted air condition at temperatures between 350 and 700 $^oC$, can yield a carbon-rich product known as Biochar.\cite{Biochar2010}
%Depending on its ash content, biochar can be used as a combustible solid for heating purposes in systems that utilize pulverized coal. Low-ash biochars have additional applications in metallurgy and serve as a source material for the production of activated carbon. Furthermore, biochar can act as a soil amendment. In agriculture sector, the removal of crops depletes substantial amounts of nutrients from the soil. If these nutrients are not replenished through organic amendments, such as biochar, the addition of synthetic fertilizers alone may not prevent a decline in soil health. Over time, this can lead to reduced cation exchange capacity, diminished water-holding capacity, and accelerated soil acidification.\cite{Biofpr2009}
%The characterization of biochar is essential to understanding its properties and optimizing its applications. Modern analytical techniques, such as Scanning electron Microscopy (SEM), Fourier-Transform Infrared Spectroscopy (FTIR), Thermogravimetric Analysis (TGA), X-ray Diffraction (XRD), Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) analysis, Nuclear Magnetic Resonance (NMR), and Raman Spectroscopy, are commonly used for this purpose.\cite{Biopro2020}
%The aim of this study is to investigate the effects of pyrolisis temperature (400, 500, and 600 $^oC$) and carbon dioxide ($CO_2$) activation on biochar produced from residual digestates obtained from recycling facilities, farms and slaughterhouses. The focus is on evaluating the properties and potential applications of the resulting biochar.
